summary: Gruber critiques Qualcomm's new ad featuring Justin Long, the actor from Apple's classic 'Get a Mac' campaign, calling it even worse than Intel's similar attempt three years prior. The ad depicts Long frustrated with his MacBook, but the supposed technical problems aren't actually real MacOS issues. Gruber contrasts these failed efforts with the original campaign's genius, arguing its success hinged on making John Hodgman's PC character lovable rather than loathsome. He notes that Apple wisely brought back Hodgman — not Long — for the Apple Silicon announcement in 2020. Gruber suggests Long has damaged his spokesperson value through serial brand betrayals, jumping from Apple to Intel to Qualcomm. The piece serves as both an ad critique and a meditation on what made the original campaign culturally enduring.
key_points: Qualcomm's Justin Long ad is even worse than Intel's 2021 version — it has no joke, bad production values, and depicts fake MacOS problems
The original 'Get a Mac' campaign succeeded because Hodgman's PC was lovable, not loathsome — making the ads funny and actually persuasive
Apple brought back Hodgman, not Long, for the Apple Silicon announcement, recognizing who the audience truly remembers
Long's serial defections (Apple → Intel → Qualcomm) damage his credibility as a spokesperson and ironically mock his previous employers
The phrase 'one more thing' is essentially sacred at Apple — Gruber believes only Jobs, Cook, and Hodgman have ever said it in keynotes
money_quotes: It's not like there's a joke here that falls flat. There is no joke, nor even an attempt at one. It's just 'Hey look, we hired the I'm a Mac guy.'
These supposed technical problems aren't actually problems on MacOS, and switching to a Snapdragon-powered Windows PC isn't going to stop his mom from emailing him.
The core genius at the heart of the original 'Get a Mac' campaign is that while Long's Mac character was likable, John Hodgman's PC — ostensibly the foil — was downright lovable.

By making Hodgman's PC the lovable-but-doomed-to-lose protagonist, the campaign wasn't just funny, it worked. It actually did what for two decades had seemed impossible — it convinced PC users to switch to the Mac.

If Apple were to work in a bit with Hodgman on screen in this Monday's WWDC keynote, the crowd at Apple Park would go bananas. If they put Long on screen, by himself, there'd be a lot of 'Who's that?'
What's next for Long, a spot in a Huawei commercial slagging on the Qualcomm modems in iPhones?
key_insight: The original 'Get a Mac' campaign endures because its creative genius was in making the rival character lovable — and competitors who hire Justin Long to mock Apple only reveal they don't understand why it worked.
